title: Optical Transmission of 2D Material with Quantum Anomalous Hall Effect
authors: Nathan Pravda, Oleg L. Berman, Klaus Ziegler
abstract: We study the optical properties of gapped two-dimensional materials which are subject to the quantum anomalous Hall effect. At sufficiently low temperatures the transmission, reflection and absorption coefficients are found to have a universal behavior that depends only on the ratio of the photonic energy and the gap energy. There is a singular behavior with total reflection when these energies are equal. In the limit of a vanishing gap we recover results for graphene, where the optical coefficients depend only on the fine-structure constant. The observed optical properties provide an accurate measurement of the bandgap.
summary: This paper explores how the Quantum Anomalous Hall effect influences the light-matter interaction in 2D materials. It identifies a sharp transition in optical transparency at the bandgap energy and demonstrates that these optical properties can be used to accurately measure the material's bandgap.
main_problem: The study investigates the frequency-dependent optical properties (transmission, reflection, and absorption) of 2D materials exhibiting the Quantum Anomalous Hall effect.
main_result: The optical response shows a universal behavior depending on the ratio of photon energy to bandgap energy, featuring a singular point of total reflection at the bandgap threshold and recovering graphene-like behavior as the gap vanishes.
method: The authors use the Kubo formalism to derive optical conductivities and apply Maxwell's equations with boundary conditions at the 2D sheet to calculate electromagnetic coefficients.
model_or_system: A two-band model of a gapped 2D material with intrinsic time-reversal symmetry breaking, characterized by longitudinal and antisymmetric Hall conductivities.
important_parameters: Scaled frequency (zeta = h-bar*omega/2m), bandgap energy (2m), and the fine-structure constant (alpha).
main_assumptions: Low temperatures where thermal fluctuations are negligible, an isotropic 2D sheet, and a focus on universal scaling rather than non-universal material parameters.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows optical conductivities vs. scaled frequency; Figure 2 displays the evolution of T, R, and A coefficients; Figure 3 illustrates the singular jump in the reflection coefficient at the threshold.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem, defines the conductivity model using the Kubo formalism, derives the electromagnetic response coefficients, analyzes different frequency regimes (insulating vs. metallic), and discusses the limits of vanishing gaps.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The optical conductivities of a 2D material in units of G0 with a band gap 2m in the presence of the QAH effect as a function of the scaled frequency ζ = ℏω/2m.
Figure 2: Transmission and absorption coefficients of a 2D material with a band gap 2m in the presence of the QAH effect and an incident electromagnetic wave with the scaled frequency ζ = ℏω/2m.
Figure 3: Singular behavior of the reflection coefficient of a 2D material with a band gap 2m in the presence of the QAH effect.
